1: ʿal pî YHWH: 'according to the mouth/command of YHWH' — the wilderness itinerary formula indicating each movement is divinely directed. The phrase recurs throughout Num 9:18-23 to describe the cloud's role in determining encampment and movement. Rĕpîdîm is the final station before Sinai; its place in the itinerary makes it the last test before the great covenant encounter.
2: wayyārēb hāʿām ʿim-mōšeh: 'the people quarreled with Moses.' The verb rîb is a legal term — it denotes filing a lawsuit, bringing a formal complaint. The same root underlies the covenant lawsuit (rîb) structure in the Prophets (Mic 6:2; Hos 4:1) where YHWH brings charges against Israel. Here Israel files a lawsuit against their deliverer. Moses's counter-question reframes the dispute: quarreling with Moses is testing (nāsāh) YHWH.
3: lāmāh zeh heʿĕlîtānû mimmîṣrāyim lĕhamît ʾōtî: 'why have you brought us up from Egypt to kill me?' The accusative ʾōtî (me) is singular — each individual speaker levels the complaint personally. The pattern of accusation — Exodus as a journey to death rather than life — repeats from 14:11 and 16:3. Each complaint ignores prior deliverance and focuses exclusively on present privation.
4: mā ʾeʿĕśeh lāʿām hazzeh: 'What shall I do with this people?' Moses's cry to YHWH is the mediator's lament caught between a rebellious people and the God they provoke. ʿÔd meʿaṭ ûsĕqālunî ('they are almost ready to stone me') — the hostility of the wilderness congregation threatens Moses personally. The mediator's vulnerability is structural: he bears the people's anger while interceding on their behalf.
5: ʿăbōr lipnê hāʿām: 'Pass on before the people.' YHWH directs Moses to take elders as witnesses — the provision of water is to occur publicly before credible witnesses. Taking the staff (maṭṭeh) used at the Nile connects this water-miracle to the Exodus plagues: the same instrument of judgment against Egypt becomes the instrument of provision for Israel.
6: hinnĕnî ʿōmēd lĕpānêkā šām ʿal-haṣṣûr bĕḥōrēb wĕhikkîtā baṣṣûr: 'I will stand before you there on the rock at Horeb, and you shall strike the rock.' The preposition ʿal (on/upon) with ʿāmad (to stand) means YHWH positions himself on the rock. The blow Moses strikes falls on the rock where YHWH stands — substitutionary solidarity. YHWH absorbs the stroke meant for covenant-breaking Israel. Ûyāṣĕʾû mimmennû mayim: 'and water shall come out of it' — not from the strike alone but from YHWH's presence on the rock. Paul's identification 'the Rock was Christ' (1 Cor 10:4) names the one who stood there to be struck so that water — life — flows for his people.
7: Massāh ûMĕrîbāh: Two place-names derived from the events: Massāh from nāsāh (to test/try) — a place of testing; Mĕrîbāh from rîb (to contend/quarrel) — a place of contention. The double name memorializes both the people's fault and YHWH's response. Ps 95:8 ('Do not harden your hearts as at Meribah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness') is cited in Heb 3:8-11 as a warning against abandoning faith.
8: wayyābōʾ ʿĂmālēq wayyillāḥem ʿim Yiśrāʾēl bĕRĕpîdîm: 'Amalek came and fought against Israel at Rephidim.' The Amalekites make their first narrative appearance here; they will become Israel's archetypal enemy (Gen 36:12; Num 24:20; Deut 25:17-19). Their attack is particularly condemned in Deut 25:18 because they attacked 'from the rear, all who were faint and weary' — they preyed on the vulnerable.
9: Yĕhôšuaʿ (Joshua) — first narrative appearance by this name (his birth name was Hôšēaʿ, Num 13:16; Moses renamed him). Yĕhôšuaʿ = YHWH saves/delivers — a theophoric name. The military commander and the intercessor are paired — Joshua's sword and Moses's raised hands are not alternatives but complements in the theology of holy war.
10: Joshua implements Moses's command; the battle begins. The narrative shifts between the valley and the hilltop to show the connection between the earthly fight and the heavenly intercession. Hur — paired with Aaron — appears here and in 24:14 (holding authority during Moses's absence on Sinai). The three-man support structure mirrors the later pattern of multiple elders sustaining a single leader's ministry.
11: kaʾăšer yārîm mōšeh yādô wĕgābar Yiśrāʾēl: 'whenever Moses raised his hand Israel prevailed.' Moses's raised hands signal sustained prayer, not magical causation. The qal participle indicates continuous action. When his hands drop, Amalek prevails — the intercession must be unbroken. Raising hands (nāśāʾ yādayim) was the posture of oath and prayer (Gen 14:22; Ps 28:2; 134:2).
12: wîdê mōšeh kĕbēdîm: 'Moses's hands were heavy.' The word kābēd (heavy) is the same root as kābôd (glory) — glory is weight. The hands too heavy to raise enact the physical analogy of weighted prayer. Aaron and Hur hold up Moses's hands — the community sustains the intercessor. The support of Moses's arms is a picture of corporate intercession: the mediator does not pray alone but is upheld by the community he represents.
13: wayyaḥălōš Yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet ʿĂmālēq: 'Joshua subdued Amalek.' The verb ḥālaš (to weaken/prostrate) conveys decisive defeat. Lĕpî ḥāreb (literally 'to the mouth of the sword') is the standard idiom for total military defeat — the sword's edge consumes. The coordinated victory — Joshua's sword below, Moses's hands above — models holy war theology: YHWH fights from his throne while his people engage in the valley.
14: kĕtōb zōʾt zikkārôn basēper: 'Write this as a memorial in the book.' The first explicit command to write in the Torah. Zikkārôn (memorial record) from zākar (to remember) — the written record ensures preservation beyond the witnessing generation. Bāsēper (in the book) with a definite article suggests a specific document, perhaps the beginning of the Mosaic literary tradition. The command to remember and erase Amalek's name provides the framework for the Purim narrative (Esther) and eschatological battle themes.
15: YHWH Nissî: 'YHWH is my banner/standard.' Moses builds an altar and names it — the practice of naming altars to commemorate YHWH's acts recurs in the patriarchal narratives (Gen 33:20; 35:7). Nēs (banner, standard) is the military insignia around which troops rally; naming YHWH as the nēs declares him the standard under which Israel fights. The same word is used in Isa 11:12: YHWH will raise a banner (nēs) for the nations.
16: yād ʿal kēs YH milḥāmāh laYHWH baʿĂmālēq middōr dōr: 'A hand is on the throne of YH — war to YHWH against Amalek from generation to generation.' The phrase is compressed and debated. Kēs may be contracted from kissēʾ (throne). YH is the shortened divine name, perhaps because the full name is withheld while Amalek's name exists. The perpetual warfare (middōr dōr = from generation to generation) establishes the theological framework for Saul's failure in 1 Sam 15 and the eschatological defeat of evil's final representative.
